The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. It checks the same precondition (1 <= number <= 1000), computes the same digit decomposition (m, c, x, i), handles m=0 or m=1 the same way, and uses the same roman_digit mapping for each digit position. The final result is constructed by concatenating the character lists and converting to a string, matching the Coq's string_of_list_ascii behavior. The roman_digit_spec function in Python correctly mirrors the Coq definition with the same digit-to-character mappings.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. It checks the same precondition (1 <= number <= 1000), computes the same digit decomposition (m, c, x, i), handles m=0 or m=1 for 'rm' identically, and uses the same roman_digit mapping for c, x, i digits. The final result is constructed by concatenating rm + rc + rx + ri and joining into a string, matching the Coq's string_of_list_ascii behavior. All concrete inputs will produce the same results.
